nodes.text: The best way to understand something is to build it from zero — implement it character by character, gradient by gradient. Abstractions are useful after you've earned them, not before.

Software 2.0: traditional software is written by humans in explicit code. The new software is written by optimization into neural network weights. This is a permanent change in how we program computers.

Neural networks are not magic — they're optimizing a loss function. A language model is doing next-token prediction. The emergent capabilities are real and fascinating, but the mechanism is concrete and studiable.

Teaching scales better than papers. A two-hour YouTube video can give ten thousand people real intuition for how transformers work. The bottleneck in AI progress is not compute or data — it's people who deeply understand what's going on.
Recipe for training neural networks: 1) become one with the data. 2) set up end-to-end training + evaluation skeleton. 3) overfit one batch. 4) regularize. 5) tune. 6) squeeze out the juice.
Stay close to the metal — C, CUDA, raw tensors. Abstractions hide the thing you need to see. Distrust layers that hide performance characteristics.
When debugging a neural network, always check the data first. Most training failures are data bugs, not model bugs.
DLinear (2023), a simple linear model, beat most transformer variants on standard time-series benchmarks — inductive bias matters more than model complexity.
LLM application hierarchy: raw prompting → few-shot → RAG → fine-tuning → pre-training. Move down the stack only when you've exhausted the level above.
nanoGPT, minGPT, llm.c — minimal, hackable reference implementations that strip ideas to their essence. Complexity is the enemy of understanding.
